revision.text:
{{日本の寺院| 名称 = 少林寺| 画像 = [[File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|260px]]
境内| 所在地 = 岡山県岡山市国富2-1-11| 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.21|N|133|56|50.52|E|scale:20000}}| 山号 = 國富山| 宗派 = [[臨済宗妙心寺派]]| 本尊 = [[観音菩薩]]| 創建年 = [[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）| 開基 = [[池田輝興]]、法隣（開山）| 正式名 = | 別称 = | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | }} '''少林寺'''（しょうりんじ）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]に所在する[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院。山号は国富山。本尊は[[観音菩薩|観世音菩薩]]。 == 概要 == この寺は[[赤穂藩]]2代藩主[[池田輝興]]が、[[兵庫県]][[赤穂市]]に建立した長松山盛岩寺を前身とする。 輝興は[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）不行状を理由に[[改易]]となり甥の[[岡山藩]]主[[池田光政]]の預かりとなった。輝興は正保4年（[[1647年]]）岡山で死去した。[[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）盛岩寺をこの地に移転し、法隣を開山和尚に迎え国富山少林寺と改称した。以後、輝興の菩提寺となった。 寺院内には[[小堀政一|小堀流]]の庭園があり、[[元禄]]12年（[[1699年]]）築庭と伝えられる。[[幕末]]の岡山藩筆頭[[家老]]であった、[[伊木忠澄]]は隠居後、三猿斎と号しこの寺院に三猿堂を結び茶の湯を楽しんだ。 == 文化財 == {{Commons|Category:Shorinji (Okayama)}} *伊木三猿斎夫妻の墓 *池田政行夫妻の墓 {{DEFAULTSORT:しようりんし}} [[Category:岡山県の寺]] [[Category:岡山市]] [[Category:中国地方の禅寺]]

{{日本の寺院| 名称 = 少林寺| 画像 = [[File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|260px]]
境内| 所在地 = 岡山県岡山市国富2-1-11| 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.21|N|133|56|50.52|E|scale:20000}}| 山号 = 國富山| 宗派 = [[臨済宗妙心寺派]]| 本尊 = [[観音菩薩]]| 創建年 = [[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）| 開基 = [[池田輝興]]、法隣（開山）| 正式名 = | 別称 = | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | }} '''少林寺'''（しょうりんじ）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]に所在する[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院。山号は国富山。本尊は[[観音菩薩|観世音菩薩]]。 == 概要 == この寺は[[赤穂藩]]2代藩主[[池田輝興]]が、[[兵庫県]][[赤穂市]]に建立した長松山盛岩寺を前身とする。 輝興は[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）不行状を理由に[[改易]]となり甥の[[岡山藩]]主[[池田光政]]の預かりとなった。輝興は正保4年（[[1647年]]）岡山で死去した。[[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）盛岩寺をこの地に移転し、法隣を開山和尚に迎え国富山少林寺と改称した。以後、輝興の菩提寺となった。 寺院内には[[小堀政一|小堀流]]の庭園があり、[[元禄]]12年（[[1699年]]）築庭と伝えられる。[[幕末]]の岡山藩筆頭[[家老]]であった、[[伊木忠澄]]は隠居後、三猿斎と号しこの寺院に三猿堂を結び茶の湯を楽しんだ。 現在、三猿斎にちなみ[[裏千家]]の茶会が定期的に催されている。 == 文化財 == {{Commons|Category:Shorinji (Okayama)}} *伊木三猿斎夫妻の墓 *池田政行夫妻の墓 {{DEFAULTSORT:しようりんし}} [[Category:岡山県の寺]] [[Category:岡山市]] [[Category:中国地方の禅寺]]

revision.text:
{{日本の寺院| 名称 = 少林寺| 画像 = [[File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|260px]]
境内| 所在地 = 岡山県岡山市中区国富2-1-11| 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.21|N|133|56|50.52|E|scale:20000}}| 山号 = 國富山| 宗派 = [[臨済宗妙心寺派]]| 本尊 = [[観音菩薩]]| 創建年 = [[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）| 開基 = [[池田輝興]]、法隣（開山）| 正式名 = | 別称 = | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | }} '''少林寺'''（しょうりんじ）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]]に所在する[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院。山号は国富山。本尊は[[観音菩薩|観世音菩薩]]。 == 概要 == この寺は[[赤穂藩]]2代藩主[[池田輝興]]が、[[兵庫県]][[赤穂市]]に建立した長松山盛岩寺を前身とする。 輝興は[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）不行状を理由に[[改易]]となり甥の[[岡山藩]]主[[池田光政]]の預かりとなった。輝興は正保4年（[[1647年]]）岡山で死去した。[[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）盛岩寺をこの地に移転し、法隣を開山和尚に迎え国富山少林寺と改称した。以後、輝興の菩提寺となった。 寺院内には[[小堀政一|小堀流]]の庭園があり、[[元禄]]12年（[[1699年]]）築庭と伝えられる。[[幕末]]の岡山藩筆頭[[家老]]であった、[[伊木忠澄]]は隠居後、三猿斎と号しこの寺院に三猿堂を結び茶の湯を楽しんだ。 現在、三猿斎にちなみ[[裏千家]]の茶会が定期的に催されている。 == 文化財 == *伊木三猿斎夫妻の墓 *池田政行夫妻の墓 == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991 16ページ {{Commons|Category:Shorinji (Okayama)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しようりんし}} [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:岡山市の寺]] [[Category:中区 (岡山市)]]

{{日本の寺院| 名称 = 少林寺| 画像 = [[File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|260px]]
境内| 所在地 = 岡山県岡山市中区国富2-1-11| 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.21|N|133|56|50.52|E|scale:20000}}| 山号 = 國富山| 宗派 = [[臨済宗妙心寺派]]| 本尊 = [[観音菩薩]]| 創建年 = [[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）| 開基 = [[池田輝興]]、法隣（開山）| 正式名 = | 別称 = | 札所等 = | 文化財 = | }} '''少林寺'''（しょうりんじ）は[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]]に所在する[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院。山号は国富山。本尊は[[観音菩薩|観世音菩薩]]。 == 概要 == この寺は[[赤穂藩]]2代藩主[[池田輝興]]が、[[兵庫県]][[赤穂市]]に建立した長松山盛岩寺を前身とする。 輝興は[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）不行状を理由に[[改易]]となり甥の[[岡山藩]]主[[池田光政]]の預かりとなった。輝興は正保4年（[[1647年]]）岡山で死去した。[[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）盛岩寺をこの地に移転し、法隣を開山和尚に迎え国富山少林寺と改称した。以後、輝興の菩提寺となった。 寺院内には[[小堀政一|小堀流]]の庭園があり、[[元禄]]12年（[[1699年]]）築庭と伝えられる。[[幕末]]の岡山藩筆頭[[家老]]であった、[[伊木忠澄]]は隠居後、三猿斎と号しこの寺院に三猿堂を結び茶の湯を楽しんだ。 現在、三猿斎にちなみ[[裏千家]]の茶会が定期的に催されている。 == 文化財 == *伊木三猿斎夫妻の墓 *池田政行夫妻の墓 == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991 16ページ {{Commons|Category:Shorinji (Okayama)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しようりんし}} [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:岡山市の寺]] [[Category:岡山市中区の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox 山 |名称 = 操山 |画像 = [[File:Misaoyama distant view.JPG|300px]] |画像キャプション = <small>Okayama Plain and Mount Misao</small> |標高 = 169 |座標 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.04|N|133|57|19.89|E|region:JP-33_scale:25000|display=it}} |所在地 = {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} |山系 = |種類 = |初登頂 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan|relief=1|coordinates={{Coord|34.660845|133.955526}}|caption=|width=300}}<small>Location of Mount Misao</small> |OSMズーム = 11 }}
'''Mount Misao''' (操山) is a low mountain located in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}, {{ill|Okayama City|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Naka Ward (Okayama City)|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}. It has an elevation of 169 meters.
When pronounced as "Sōzan," it refers to the northwestern area of the mountain.
In the past, it was also written as '''Sansōzan''' or '''Sankaiyama'''.
== Overview == Mount Misao is a low mountain located in the eastern part of Okayama City and serves as borrowed scenery for {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}}. The highest point is 169 meters, with {{ill|Maruyama (Okayama)|ja|円山}} (138m) and {{ill|Kasaiyama|ja|笠井山}} (134m) extending to the east.
Historically, Mount Misao was called "Mikaiyama" (瓶井山). During the Edo period, it became known as "Sankaiyama" or "Sansōzan," and later, the name "Misaozan" (操山) became standard. It was a site of ancient rituals, with {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rocks) and numerous {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} (ancient tombs), forming the {{ill|Misao Kofun Group|ja|操山古墳群}}.
At the foot of the mountain, several religious sites are scattered, including {{ill|Sōgen-ji (Okayama)|ja|曹源寺 (岡山市)}}, Busshin-ji, {{ill|Okayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}}, {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|ja|カナメモチ}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|ja|少林寺 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Anju-in|ja|安住院}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|ja|恩徳寺 (岡山市)}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|ja|吉備津岡辛木神社}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|ja|明善寺合戦}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|ja|国有林}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|ja|和気清麻呂}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|ja|楠木正行}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|ja|児島高徳}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|ja|玉井宮東照宮}}.

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

revision.text:
{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

revision.text:
{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en}}[[ceb:カナメモチ]] File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office
== See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en}}[[ja:大西祝]] - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the qqth year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the qqth year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the qqth year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en}}[[ceb:カナメモチ]] File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office== See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en}}[[ja:大西祝]] - A philosopher from Okayama

After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]], {{ill|Anju-in|en}}[[ja:安住院]], and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}[[ja:恩徳寺 (岡山市)]]. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en}}[[ja:吉備津岡辛木神社]], Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}[[ja:明善寺合戦]].
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en}}[[ja:岡山藩]], later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}}[[ja:国有林]] after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en}}[[ja:和気清麻呂]], {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}[[ja:楠木正行]], and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en}}[[ja:児島高徳]]. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}[[ja:近代社格制度]]. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en}}[[ja:玉井宮東照宮]].

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|12=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|en}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=draft}}.

{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

revision.comment: 19 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:少林寺 (岡山市) up to rev 87418871
revision.text:
{{日本の寺院 | 名称 = 少林寺 | 画像 = [[File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|260px]] | 画像説明 = 境内 | 所在地 = [[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]][[国富 (岡山市)|国富]]2-1-11 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|39.21|N|133|56|50.52|E|scale:20000}} | 山号 = 國富山（国富山） | 院号 = | 寺号 = 少林寺 | 宗旨 = [[臨済宗]] | 宗派 = [[臨済宗妙心寺派|妙心寺派]]（[[妙心寺]]末） | 本尊 = [[観音菩薩]] | 創建年 = [[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]） | 開基 = [[池田輝興]]、[[法隣]]（開山） | 正式名 = 國富山少林寺
国富山少林寺 | 別称 = | 札所等 = | 文化財 = }} '''少林寺'''（しょうりんじ）は、[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]][[国富 (岡山市)|国富]]に所在する[[臨済宗妙心寺派]]の寺院。山号は国富山。本尊は[[観音菩薩|観世音菩薩]]。 == 概要 == この寺は[[赤穂藩]]2代藩主[[池田輝興]]が、[[兵庫県]][[赤穂市]]に建立した'''長松山盛岩寺'''を前身とする。輝興は[[正保]]2年（[[1645年]]）不行状を理由に[[改易]]となり、甥の[[岡山藩]]主[[池田光政]]の預かりとなった。輝興は正保4年（[[1647年]]）に岡山で死去した。[[万治]]3年（[[1660年]]）、盛岩寺をこの地に移転し、法隣を開山和尚に迎え、国富山少林寺と改称した。以後、輝興の菩提寺となった。 寺院内には[[小堀政一|小堀流]]の庭園があり、[[元禄]]12年（[[1699年]]）築庭と伝えられる。[[幕末]]の岡山藩筆頭[[家老]]であった[[伊木忠澄]]は隠居後に三猿斎と号し、この寺院に三猿堂を結び茶の湯を楽しんだ。 現在、三猿斎にちなみ、[[裏千家]]の茶会が定期的に催されている。 == 文化財 == *伊木三猿斎夫妻の墓 *池田政行夫妻の墓 == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会/編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991 16ページ {{Commonscat|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しようりんし}} [[Category:臨済宗妙心寺派の寺院]] [[Category:岡山市の寺]] [[Category:岡山市中区の歴史]] [[Category:岡山市中区の建築物]] [[Category:岡山藩]] [[Category:赤穂池田家|寺]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[少林寺 (岡山市)]] to [[Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

revision.text:
{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]

== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

revision.text:
{{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|qid=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|qid=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
