<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]
==See Also== * [[Minato Chausuyama Kofun]] ...t the summit of a hill in the southern portion of the {{ill|Mount Misao|lt=Mount Misao|lt=Sozan Hills|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sozan Hills|qq=}}, located on the eastern bank of the {{ill|Asahi River (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi River (Okayama)|qq=}} i ... 5 KB (707 words) - 01:04,

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]

It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11464758]] Wikidata name: Shōrin-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]
==See Also== * [[Minato Chausuyama Kofun]] ...t the summit of a hill in the southern portion of the {{ill|Mount Misao|lt=Mount Misao|lt=Sozan Hills|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sozan Hills|qq=}}, located on the eastern bank of the {{ill|Asahi River (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi River (Okayama)|qq=}} i ... 5 KB (707 words) - 01:04,
2025

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama

It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Shōrin-ji (Okayama)]][[ja:少林寺 (岡山市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Shorinji, Okayama 05.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Okayama|WD=Q200078|1=Okayama}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.6608917|N|133.9473667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}} {{ill|Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}, {{ill|Anju-in|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anju-in|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ontoku-ji (Okayama)|qq=}}. The hills also house {{ill|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine|qq=}}, Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō, the ruins of Sankun Shrine, and the remains of {{ill|Myōzenji Castle|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōzenji Castle|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, it was a forest owned by the {{ill|Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain|2=ja|3=岡山藩|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Domain|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903497}}, later becoming a {{ill|State forest|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State forest|qq=}} after the Meiji era. Today, the area is maintained as Misao Park with walking trails.
== Misao Park Satoyama Center == Built in the 11th year of the Heisei era (1999), this facility promotes learning about Satoyama (village forests). It provides information, books, and exhibitions about Mount Misao, as well as interactive spaces, conference rooms, and a multi-purpose hall. Surrounding the center are open areas, a charcoal-burning hut, a farm, and an orchard. Various events, such as nature observation and history lectures, are held here.
== Sankun Shrine Ruins == '''Sankun Shrine''' (三勲神社) was located at the end of a ridge extending west from Mount Misao. Established in 1875 (Meiji 8) under national policy and local initiatives, it was built at a site overlooking Okayama City. The deities enshrined were {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wake no Kiyomaro|qq=}}, {{ill|Kusunoki Masatsura|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusunoki Masatsura|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kojima Takanori|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Takanori|qq=}}. It held the status of a prefectural shrine under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|qq=}}. As it was created by volunteers, there were no shrine parishioners. After World War II, the abolition of the modern shrine Ranking system made its operation unfeasible, and in 1947 (Shōwa 22), the shrine was relocated to the precincts of {{ill|Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamai-gu Tōshō-gū|qq=}}.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Misaoyama Panoramic View.JPG|Panoramic view of the Misao hills File:Misaoyama 09.JPG|Summit of Mount Misao File:Maruyama Top.JPG|Summit of Maruyama File:Kasaiyama Top.JPG|Summit of Kasaiyama File:Misaoyama Koen Satoyama Center 01.JPG|Misao Park Satoyama Center File:Misaoyama 03.JPG|Walking trail File:Misaoyama 10.JPG|Tunnel path of {{ill|Photinia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Photinia|qq=}} File:Misaoyama 08.JPG|Bamboo forest walking trail File:Sankun Jinja remains.JPG|Ruins of Sankun Shrine File:Misaoyama 07.JPG|Maruyama Fudō Myō-ō File:Kibitsuokakaraki Jinja 02.JPG|Kibitsu Okakaraki Shrine File:Myozenji Castle Honmaru.JPG|Ruins of Myōzenji Castle File:Okayama city view from Sankunjinja remains.JPG|View of Okayama City from Sankun Shrine ruins File:Misaoyama 05.JPG|View of Okayama Plain from the Flag Signal Observatory </gallery>
== References == * ''Kibi no Kuni Temple Tour 2011 Edition,'' Sanyo Shimbun Publishing, 2011 * ''Misao Park Satoyama Center Pamphlet,'' Okayama City Parks Association * ''Shingon Buddhism Mikaiyama Zenko-ji Anju-in,'' pamphlet published by the temple * On-site explanation boards by Okayama City and the Okayama Forestry Management Office == See Also == * {{ill|Ōnishi Isao|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnishi Isao|qq=}} - A philosopher from Okayama
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Mount Misao]] * [http://www.okayama-park.or.jp/ Okayama City Parks Association]
==See Also== * [[Minato Chausuyama Kofun]] ...t the summit of a hill in the southern portion of the {{ill|Mount Misao|lt=Mount Misao|lt=Sozan Hills|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sozan Hills|qq=}}, located on the eastern bank of the {{ill|Asahi River (Okayama)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asahi River (Okayama)|qq=}} i ... 5 KB (707 words) - 01:04,
